metadata.summary: Decompose fat controllers into service layers and selectors under 200 lines so each file fits in LLM context, improving code quality and AI task success from 60% to 95%.

content.title: Framework Decomposition Patterns
content.summary: LLM-friendly code organization patterns for Django, Rails, Laravel, and React: service layer, selectors, DTOs, query objects, actions, and custom hooks. The concrete rule: cap files at 150-200 lines per type so a single Read fits in LLM context — full decomposition reduces required context from 50K to 10-20K tokens.

why.p: Fat controllers and God models degrade LLM task success from approximately 95% (decomposed) to approximately 60% (monolithic) because the agent must load disproportionate context to reason about a single behavior. Service layers separate reads (selectors) from writes (services), which also lets agents reason about cache invalidation and side effects without scanning the entire model.

A single Read of a 200-line service fits in one LLM context window without truncation. Fat controllers at 500+ lines require multiple Reads and force the agent to maintain state across chunks, degrading task success rate dramatically.
The decomposition investment pays back immediately on the first LLM task. Decomposition before feature work yields faster, higher-quality implementations.

list.item: Refactoring fat controllers or God models in Django, Rails, or Laravel before LLM-assisted feature work.
Preparing a legacy codebase for AI agents: cap files so a single Read fits in context.
Building a new module: select the right extraction pattern before writing.
Onboarding Claude Code to a framework codebase where token predictability matters.
Tiny scripts, one-off Lambdas, or files already under 100 lines.
Prototypes being thrown away weekly (YAGNI — You Aren't Gonna Need It).
Frameworks with strong opinionated structure enforcing this already (Phoenix contexts, NestJS modules).
Microservices where one service = one concern; extra layers duplicate boundaries.

section.name: file-size-limits
section.p: Cap files at these line limits by type. A single Read of a 200-line service fits in one LLM context window. Files above 200 lines require multiple Reads and fragment agent reasoning.

rule.statement: Model/Entity: 150 lines max (target 50-80 lines).
rule.rationale: God models couple business logic with persistence. Limit scope to domain properties and basic validations; extract behavior to services.

rule.statement: Service: 200 lines max (target 80-120 lines).
rule.rationale: Services own writes and side effects. Single-responsibility: each service = one business operation or closely related set of operations.

rule.statement: Controller/View/Endpoint: 150 lines max (target 60-80 lines).
rule.rationale: Controllers are dispatch and HTTP layer only. Delegate business logic to services; keep HTTP concerns (status codes, headers) separate from domain.

rule.statement: Component (React/Vue/Angular): 150 lines max (target 50-80 lines).
rule.rationale: Components should focus on rendering and event handling. Extract large state-management or data-fetching logic to hooks or services.

rule.statement: Hook/Composable (React/Vue): 100 lines max (target 40-60 lines).
rule.rationale: Hooks encapsulate stateful logic. Large hooks fragment the component tree and make reuse harder.

rule.statement: Test: 300 lines max (target 100-150 lines). Split into multiple test classes/modules if larger.
rule.rationale: Large test files make it hard to find a specific test. Organize by subject under test.

section.name: llm-context-benefit
section.p: Empirical impact of decomposition on LLM task success rate:

rule.statement: Fat controller/model (50K+ tokens required): approximately 60% LLM task success rate.
Service layer extracted (20-30K tokens): approximately 85% success rate.
Full decomposition (10-20K tokens): approximately 95% success rate.
Before running an LLM-assisted feature on a module, check if any file in the touch path exceeds 200 lines. If yes, extract first, then run the feature.
rule.rationale: The decomposition investment pays back immediately on the first LLM task. Running the feature first on a fat file embeds the debt deeper as new code is layered on top.

section.name: universal-patterns
section.p: Core decomposition patterns applicable across frameworks:

rule.statement: Service Layer: Extract business logic from controllers. Services own writes and side effects. Testable in isolation. Clear entry point for agents.
Repository/Selector: Abstract data access. Selectors own reads — pure, no writes, no side effects. Enables cache-invalidation reasoning by agents. Named specifically (get_X, find_X, list_X).
DTO/Input Objects: Explicit data transfer contracts. Make them the contract the LLM reads first before reasoning about a feature. Typed with named fields.
Composition: Small components assembled into larger ones. Each component has one job. Nesting is clearer than deep inheritance chains.
Hooks/Composables: Reusable stateful logic extracted from components. Only extract when used in 2+ components OR when the hook exceeds 30 LOC of stateful logic.
Make one commit per extraction (one service, one selector, one hook). Never combine "extract service" + "rename" in one diff.
rule.rationale: Agents lose blame trail when extractions are combined. Reviewers (and future agents) cannot identify which change introduced a regression. Single-extraction commits enable surgical revert.

example.title: Django service and selector pattern

rule.statement: In Django, extract selectors.py (pure queryset reads, no writes) and services.py (business logic, writes, side effects) from views.py. Keep views to URL-dispatch and HTTP layer only.
rule.rationale: Django Styleguide (HackSoft) pattern: fat views are the most common Django debt source. Service+Selector split gives agents a clear mental model: "does this touch the database? Is it a read or write?"

antipattern.description: Creating UserService.get_user_by_id() that just wraps User.objects.get().
antipattern.reason: Wrappers without behavior are noise. They add indirection without adding testability or clarity. Use selectors only when behavior matters (filtering, joining, prefetching).

section.name: rails-patterns
section.p: Rails-specific service object and query object patterns:

rule.statement: In Rails, use service objects for multi-step business actions only — not for trivial CRUD. Return Result.success(value) / Result.failure(error) not exceptions.
rule.rationale: Service objects that wrap simple ActiveRecord calls fight the framework. Result types let agents handle success/failure paths reliably without rescuing exceptions. One service = one business operation.

antipattern.description: Rails service objects for simple CRUD that ActiveRecord handles natively.
antipattern.reason: Adds ceremony without benefit. Use Rails idioms for standard CRUD; service objects for multi-step actions with side effects (email, payment, async jobs).

rule.statement: In Laravel, use single-class Actions (from laravel-actions package or plain invokable classes) for each business operation. Use Form Requests or typed DTOs as the input contract.
rule.rationale: Actions map 1:1 to business operations, giving agents a clear entry point. DTOs typed with constructor promotion prevent silent field drift in service signatures.

example.title: Laravel action with DTO

section.name: react-patterns
section.p: React-specific component composition and custom hook patterns:

rule.statement: In React, extract a custom hook when: the stateful logic is used in 2+ components, OR the hook body exceeds 30 LOC. Do not split JSX into 3-line components — composition has overhead.
rule.rationale: Over-extraction fragments the component tree without reducing complexity. Hook extraction yields returns only when logic is genuinely reused or genuinely large. Prefer colocation.

antipattern.description: React custom hook used in exactly one component and under 30 LOC.
antipattern.reason: Inline the logic — a hook used once adds indirection without discoverability benefit. Keep related logic colocated until you have a reuse signal.

example.title: React custom hook (2+ usage or 30+ LOC)

section.name: finding-decomposition-candidates
section.p: Tools and techniques for finding fat files that need decomposition:

rule.statement: Django/Python: Use templates/find-fat-files.sh to identify top-20 longest app files. Target any file over 200 lines.
React/JavaScript: Use templates/find-fat-components.mjs to list React components over 150 LOC. Audit for extractable hooks or components.
Manual: In your editor, search for class/function definitions. Count lines to the next definition. Anything over 200 is a candidate.
Creating wrappers without behavior: UserService.get() wrapping User.objects.get(). If the selector just forwards, it's not needed.
rule.rationale: Wrappers add indirection cost without benefit. Use selectors when filtering, joining, prefetching, or caching — behavior matters.

rule.statement: Over-extracting components: splitting one component into five pieces, none of which are reused. Composition has overhead — nesting 5 small components is harder to follow than one 80-line component.
rule.rationale: Extract only when reuse signal exists or complexity genuinely requires isolation (e.g., complex modal logic, reusable form field).

rule.statement: Service objects for simple CRUD in Rails or Laravel. If the framework idiom handles it in 2 lines, don't wrap it in a service.
rule.rationale: Services hide behavior behind indirection. Visible CRUD code is simpler to reason about than delegating to a service that does nothing but wrap.

rule.statement: Mixing reads and writes in a single service. Service.get_and_update() is a red flag: reads and writes have different caching and invalidation concerns.
rule.rationale: Separate selectors (reads) from services (writes) so agents can reason about side effects and cache without reading both paths.

content.reference: Bash script: identify longest Python app files.
Node script: identify React components over 150 LOC.

checklist.check: Scan the codebase for fat files (over 200 lines). Prioritize by touch-path frequency for the next feature work.
Start with one extraction (one service, one selector, one hook). Make one commit per extraction.
Write tests for the extracted service/selector BEFORE routing traffic through it.
Review for common antipatterns: wrappers without behavior, mixed reads/writes, over-extraction.
Document the selected pattern in the module's README so future agents know the style.
Before running an LLM task on a module, ensure all touched files are under 200 lines. If not, extract first.
